data.title: Caterpillar Q4 Preview: Backlog, Margins, And The AI Power Trade
data.description:
I initiate coverage on Caterpillar Inc. (CAT) as the company is about to release one of its most important earnings reports in recent times on January 29th.

After the Q3 2025 print, the stock rerated from a late-cycle boring industrial play to an AI picks-and-shovels beneficiary, similar to GE Vernova (GEV) and other power generation manufacturers last year, with backlogs tied to the AI data center buildout.

In the upcoming earnings release, I will be looking for signs of confirmation that engine demand for data center applications was not a one-quarter spike in Q3. The key factor to monitor is backlog, particularly the sequential increase between Q3 and Q4. As a side note, I'm looking for at least a 10% QOQ increase to validate my AI data center power demand point.

I will also look for any signs of stabilization in profitability. Here, the FY26 guidance on tariffs will be key. So far, the FY25 range sits between $1.6B-$1.75B after being upgraded in Q3.

Overall, I think the market may be willing to look past the margin compression, especially in the construction and resources segments, if the Q4 backlog shows a significant increase. I don't see any signs of a slowdown in the AI data center buildout among the hyperscalers. I would monitor Meta's (META) and Microsoft's (MSFT) remarks this Wednesday, but the chances of a slowdown in the data center buildout are slim. That's why I believe backlog could see a spike in Q4, followed by upward analyst revisions.

The Re-Rate Story

Back in Q3, investors’ perception of Caterpillar changed drastically. It quickly went from a heavy equipment manufacturer at the peak of the cycle to an AI picks-and-shovels play.

The Power Generation product line is the key pillar of the narrative surrounding this stock. In Q3 2025, the Energy & Transportation segment reported an increase of 25% in sales to users, with Power Generation up 33%.

What was the main driver?

Well, if you are tuned in with GE Vernova, you may have guessed this one correctly: demand for reciprocating engines for data center applications. These mainly include diesel and gas generators like the ones below.

That said, the product line expands far beyond these massive generators. Some of the data center offerings include (see links below if you are interested in the product line):

  • Battery Energy Storage Systems.

  • Power controls, switchgear/paralleling, and automatic transfer switches.

  • Integrated power systems configurations.

The latest slide deck shows the different global megatrends that the company is exposed to, and the 200% projected increase in data center electricity demand stands out the most.

One of the metrics that drove the re-rate post Q3 was backlog. In Q3 alone, the company reported a jump of $2.4B in backlog, up 39% YOY. As seen below, the backlog growth over the past 12 months is quite impressive.

Q4 Earnings Preview

Without any doubt, the key segment to track in the upcoming earnings release is the performance of the Energy & Transportation segment. Back in Q3, E&T sales increased by 17% YOY. This comes after a significant slowdown in growth since mid-2023. Notice the spike in growth in Q3 in the chart below:

Profitability in this segment is another factor to consider. Take a look at the inflection point in Q3 in the operating profit of this segment.

Aside from the AI story premium, this segment is the most important one from a revenue contribution perspective. In Q3, sales in E&T represented 48% of total revenue, followed by the construction industries segment at 38% of total revenue.

From a guidance perspective, the recent spike in data center demand led management to raise FY revenue targets. From a revenue perspective, back in Q2, management said that revenue should be slightly higher YOY. In Q3, the revenue guidance used the term modestly higher. Unfortunately, the management of this company is way too conservative and didn't provide a concrete value. Where they did provide more color is on the FCF guidance for the E&T segment. Back in Q2, the expectation was around the middle of the $5-10B range. The new expectations moved above the midpoint of the previous target range:

Finally, on profits, I noticed a downgrade in full-year guidance. Before Q3, the expectation was within the bottom half of the annual target range. As a side note, the company models that range based on realized annual revenue:

In Q3, that expectation moved to near the bottom of that range, which brings me to the next point.

Risks

The main factor that makes me sweat with my Strong Buy rating is the compression in operating profits shown in the chart below:

For reference, Q3 adjusted operating margin was down to 17.5% from 20.0% a year ago. The two segments that are being pressured here are construction (3 pts down YOY) and resources (4.3 pts down YOY). The latter segment is quite disappointing, with low single-digit growth in Q3:

The main driver seems to be tariff costs. Back in Q2, the company estimated the net incremental tariff costs to be between $1.3B and $1.5B. That range was upgraded in Q3 to $1.6B - $1.75B. Therefore, another key metric to monitor in the upcoming release is the tariff costs guidance for FY26.

In my view, the company has limited power in passing the costs through to end consumers. I believe the company may be near a peak cycle, particularly in the construction segment. In the last earnings release, management guided Q4 2025 machine dealer inventory to decline slightly. Bear in mind that back in Q4 2024, the drop was $1.6B.

In the upcoming earnings call, I will closely monitor any comments on dealer destocking. Even though Q3 showed an increase in dealer inventory, I don't think the company is out of the woods yet until I see margins recovering.

Valuation

Caterpillar used to be eye candy for value-based investors. This drastically changed after the stock was re-rated as an AI picks and shovels play.

The stock is trading at 34x next year's earnings. Seeking Alpha rates this multiple with a D+, given that it is 33% above the sector median and 79% of the 5-year company average.

However, I think paying 34x for next year's earnings is cheap. Why?

Simply because its main competitor in the power generation space, GE Vernova, is trading at a sky-high multiple of 85x.

Conclusion

Overall, I believe there is room for further multiple expansion if Q4 results validate the AI data center narrative. I believe there are two items that will decide whether the Q3 re-rate holds or reverses.

First, the Q4 print needs to confirm that the data center engine demand is not a one-quarter spike. I would closely monitor the growth of the E&T segment, particularly the power generation product line, which was up 33% YOY in Q3. The growth in backlog is another item worth monitoring. The sequential increase between Q2 and Q3 was approximately 6%. I am looking for a double-digit sequential increase in Q4.

Second, with Q3 adjusted operating margin falling to 17.5% from 20.0% a year ago, I would closely monitor the performance of the construction and resources segments. These two segments dragged the overall profitability of the company due to the impact of tariffs and the limited capacity to pass these costs to customers. From this perspective, management's FY26 tariff costs guidance is the key item to monitor.

Overall, I feel cautiously optimistic heading into the print. If the guidance management provides in Q4 is favorable, I believe there is a good chance we could see another wave of analyst upward revisions, similar to Q3.

It is my view that shares tend to track the analyst consensus (see below). Therefore, I see more room for multiple expansion.

data.title: Gold Up, Dollar Down
data.description:
What does this week seem to hold for investors?

Gold prices are up...the U.S. dollar is down.

This market behavior is getting more and more attention.

And this seems to be depicting the feelings about the future.

Here, let's look at where we are.

One year ago, the price of gold was around $2,740.00 per ounce.

Monday morning, January 26, 2026, the price of gold was around $5,090.00 per ounce.

Year-over-year increase: right around 85 percent.

A pretty nice gain.

The value of the U.S. dollar.

The DXY, U.S. dollar index, was right around 108.00 one year ago. Monday morning, January 26, 2026, the index was around 97.00.

The year-over-year drop in the index was right around 10 percent.

A fairly substantial decline.

Ronnie Harui and Fabiana Negrin Ochoa comment on the current situation in the Wall Street Journal:

"Fraying confidence in the dollar is also supporting precious metal prices, with fresh highs offering further evidence of a deepening crisis of confidence in the U.S. dollar and the broader monetary order."

They further write that

"Goldman Sachs analysts last week raised their forecast for gold to reach $5,400 an ounce by the end of the year, up from $4,900 an ounce previously, citing central bank buying and private sector diversification into gold."

Mention is also made about "surging" silver prices.

The point is that "Investors (have) continued seeking refuge in haven assets..."

This is a real market response to the fact that "A Year In, Trump's Economy Is Worse Than The Numbers."

These are the headlines of a commentary written by Alan Blinder, professor of economics at Princeton University and former vice chairman of the Federal Reserve from 1994-1996.

Blinder presents the current Trump review of the scene:

"Insisting that he inherited a mess from Joe Biden, the president claims that it's now the greatest economy America has ever seen. 'We have gone from the worst numbers on record to the best.'"

To this, Mr. Blinder adds:

"There are two problems with that claim: Neither part is true."

Then, Blinder reviews the statistics.

Something is going on.

We are getting some warnings.

The stock market is up again on Monday morning, but we have not had any more historic highs since Monday morning, January 12.

The investment community is trying to comprehend where things are with respect to the state of the U.S. economy and where the policymakers are.

A lot of uncertainty is in the air.

Volatility has increased.

This is causing some investors to seek "safety."

And what is it that might be lurking in the shadows, something that might upset this picture of stability and tranquility that has been the case in the financial markets for the past almost four years?

The answer I would give is that there is plenty of money still around in the economy, money that could turn into inflationary pressures even though the economy is not growing excessively fast.

Alan Blinder covers this problem with these words:

"The consumer price index for the 12 months ending December 2025 was 2.7 percent--compared with 2.9 percent over the 12 months ending December 2024."

"Progress?"

"I guess so, though a little more than a rounding error."

"And healthcare costs are poised to soar for millions of Americans in 2026."

"Not exactly the achievement of 'almost no inflation' the president boasts about."

"Has he visited a grocery store?"

This is where the investor needs to watch out.

When we get a little bit away from all the "good news" we are hearing from the president, we find some things that make us uncomfortable.

These "things" that are making us uncomfortable are apparently making some other investors uncomfortable.

The news from the "gold" market...and from the "silver" market...and from the "dollar" market...is unnerving.

Looking a little bit further, we see something else that is a little bit unnerving.

There is a lot of money sitting around in the banking system.

Take a look at this....

Over the past five years, the M2 money stock has risen at a compound rate of around 6.5 percent.

The real economy has been growing, year-over-year, in the 2.0% to 2.5% range.

If the real rate of economic growth continues at this rate for the next four or five years and the M2 rate of growth of the M2 monetary stock remains around 6.5 percent, what's to happen with inflation?

The turnover (velocity) of the M2 money stock has shown this behavior over the past three years.

With a growth rate in the M2 money stock of 6.5 percent and a rate of growth in the real economy of 2.5 percent (high side of forecast) and a "steady" monetary velocity, the estimate for inflation comes in at 4.0 percent!

With these figures about the future...it is no wonder that many investors are seeing a continued fall in the value of the U.S. dollar and...if this is not controlled, it is no wonder that investors are putting more and more of their funds...into gold!!!

So, keep your eyes on the value of the U.S. dollar.

Keep your eyes on the price of gold.

These are important indicators for the future!

data.title: My Transition From Ray Dalio's 4-Asset Portfolio To The Barbell Allocation
data.description:
Dalio’s All-Weather Portfolio and the New Monetary Order

For decades, investors – this author himself include – following Ray Dalio’s 4-asset allocation (aka the All-Weather Portfolio, AWP) have been rewarded by the disciplined approach. As a background for readers unfamiliar with this model, the AWP is a risk-parity model designed to perform across four economic seasons: rising growth, falling growth, rising inflation, and falling inflation. It typically utilizes a mix of 30% stocks, 40% long-term Treasuries, 15% intermediate Treasuries, 7.5% gold, and 7.5% commodities. The allocation could surprise many investors given the relatively heavy weight of treasury bonds (55% in total) and the low weight of equity (only 30% stock). The reason is that the goal of the AWP is to balance the risk contribution rather than just the dollar amount, and the strategy has worked beautifully for decades, as seen in the next chart. The AWP not only outperformed the SP500 for extended periods of time but also achieved the outperformance with far lower drawdowns.

However, as investors, we have to always bear in mind that past performance does not guarantee future success and keep an open mind as market conditions evolve. When facts change, we have to change our mind. I started to rethink the effectiveness of the AWP around 2020, when treasury rates dropped to historically low levels (see the next chart below). If I—or you—kept following the 55% allocation towards treasuries under those conditions (when 10-year treasury rates dropped below 1%), my portfolio return would be quite poor, as reflected by the large lag between AWP and SP500 shown in the chart above. The macroeconomic developments since then and the recent comments provided by Ray Dalio himself regarding the “new monetary order” further convinced me that the lag is likely to continue.

Against this background, the goals for the rest of this article are twofold. Firstly, I want to explain why the AWP worked so well in the past. Secondly and more importantly, I will identify the top reasons for AWP’s effectiveness to diminish in the future and encourage investors to consider a barbell model instead under the new monetary order.

Dalio’s AWP Needs a Revisit

Beside the fundamental soundness of the approach, a key reason for AWP’s past success is the secular decline of US treasury rates as shown in the chart above. From my reading, the concepts behind the AWP were documented by Bridgewater Associates in the early 1990s with roots in economic observations from the 1970s, a period with very elevated US treasury rates. As seen, 10-year treasury rates hovered around 8% on average in the 1970s and peaked at ~16% in the early 1980s. The rates then followed a decades-long downward trend, dropping below 1% during the epic easing amid the COVID pandemic, as just mentioned. A 15% decline in treasury rates translates to a 150% price return already for 10-year bonds and more on bonds with longer duration (and the AWP specifically calls for 40% of treasuries with extended duration). The total return would be even better when the mouthwatering coupons are factored in - and even better when reinvested.

Keep peeling the onion; a key reason beyond the decades-long bull run of the treasury bonds is investors’—not ’only in the US but globally—confidence in fiat currencies (especially US dollars) and US debt as reliable stores of wealth. But this has changed and will keep changing in the eyes of Dalio. An example of his changing view is quoted below:

Seeking Alpha News: Ray Dalio, founder of Bridgewater Associates, has declared that the global monetary order is actively breaking down, citing a fundamental shift away from fiat currencies and debt as reliable stores of wealth. “The monetary order is breaking down,” Dalio said in an interview with CNBC at Davos. “What I mean by the monetary order is that fiat currencies and debt as a store of wealth is not being held by central banks in the same way.” The billionaire investor emphasized that holders of U.S. dollar-denominated debt and the United States itself are growing increasingly wary of each other, creating a dangerous dynamic.

I totally share the above thinking, which is also well supported by data. Two examples are shown below. The first example is provided by the Federal Reserve. According to its data, the U.S. dollar currently comprises 58 percent of disclosed global official foreign reserves in 2024 (the latest available data). The weight has been in consistent decline from its peak of 72 percent of reserves in 2001, as seen. The second example is provided by the IMF (International Monetary Fund), which shows that when adjusted for exchange rates and interest rates, the decline is even more dramatic than the notional figure reported.

What Does a Barbell Allocation Look Like?

In response to the above outlook, Dalio recommended investors maintain between 5% and 15% allocation to gold. Note that this recommendation suggests a far larger allocation toward gold (up to twice as much) than in the original AWP’s 7.5% target allocation. When gold takes, something else has to give. And I recommend investors scale back their exposure to US Treasury bonds to increase their gold exposure if they want to follow Dalio’s new guidelines. There are certainly macroscopic considerations behind this notion. As an example, recent geopolitical developments (such as those involving Venezuela and Greenland) have led me to see that other countries, even the US’s allies, become more wary of holding US debt. However, I am in no position to assess these macroscopic forces better than Dalio and I will just encourage you to read his comments.

At a more microscopic and practical level, I feel confident in this forecast: substantial exposure to US treasuries under current conditions will lead to underperformance in the years to come. Among the many reasons behind this forecast, let me limit myself to the top 2 here. First, unlike in the 1970s or 1980s, treasury bonds offer far more limited potential for upsides. Currently 10-year treasury rates are ~4.2%. Even if they drop to 1% again like in the COVID pandemic—the only time this has ever happened to my knowledge—it will cause a 32% price gain for 10-year treasury bonds. This leads to my second reason: the role of inflation and real rates. A gain of 32% sounds decent on the surface but is nothing but given the current inflation and real rates. As you can see from the following chart, the current real US treasury rates are A) negative and B) also near the lowest levels since the 1970s when adjusted for inflation.

If you do increase exposure to hard currency (such as gold) and decrease exposure to bonds, the portfolio essentially morphs from an AWP to a barbell model in my view. For readers new to the concept, a barbell allocation moves away from the conventional wisdom of a middle ground or a “balanced” mix of multiple assets. Instead, it focuses on the two extremes of the risk spectrum: one end is comprised of ultra-safe and liquid assets (such as gold or even cash), while the other end is dedicated to high-risk, high-reward aggressive growth assets, such as exposure to technology stocks.

Other Risks and Final Thoughts

Dalio’s own tactical position changes echo the above shift. For example, besides a shift toward gold, he also expressed optimism about technological innovation, with parts of his portfolio invested in "new tech creating great disruptions."

For me, I have gone a step further towards the barbell model. Over the years, I have gradually divided my investible funds into two parts. Not necessarily equal parts, and their sizes change with market conditions and my financial needs. I invest each part in the two extremes of the risk curve - kind of the two ends of a barbell—and avoid the middle risk spectrum. In the low-risk extreme (what I call a survival portfolio), I hold sizable cash, gold, and defensive stocks. The goal there is NOT growth or alpha, but survival even under extreme market corrections. With survival assured, I can allocate the second parts in the high-risk extreme to capture growth. My experience with this approach over the years has been successful. This model has helped me to generalize alpha with clearly isolated risks. As an example, the next two charts below show my growth portfolio’s holdings and also performance since I started posting it regularly as part of our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) in 2022. As seen, my allocation to bonds totals about 24%, far below the 55% recommended in the AWP. And my core equity holdings include bets more aggressive than the S&P 500 with exposure to leveraged ETFs such as TQQQ and small-cap funds such as XBI.

Before closing, I wanted to caution investors about the downside risks and limitations of the barbell model, as there is no silver bullet in investing. First, the survival extreme of the barbell is always a moving target. Its goal is to provide funds to ensure our needs under all circumstances, but our needs change with factors and considerations beyond investing (such as our other income sources and our living arrangements). Second, the barbell model can heighten volatility risks (or at least give investors the impression of heightened volatility risks) because the growth portfolio could suffer drawdowns far worse than a portfolio built around the middle ground. As such, investors should tailor their own risk tolerance and financial needs before embracing the barbell with both arms.

To conclude, my thesis for this article is mainly twofold. First, I wanted to explain why the 4-asset allocation worked so effectively in the past. Second, I want to argue why some of the key drivers behind its historical performance have changed structurally. For these reasons, we feel this is timely for investors to rethink the AWP under current market conditions and consider some alternative allocation methods such as the barbell model.

data.title: If I Could Own Only 3 Dividend Growth Stocks For The Long-Term
data.description:
When it comes to evaluating stocks and how likely they are to outperform over time, I generally divide them into three buckets.

In the short term, valuation multiple expansion tends to play the biggest role in determining which stocks outperform others. This is simply because a company's yield plus growth is fairly constrained over short periods of time. Therefore, it can only have a limited impact on the total returns it generates. However, valuation multiples can change dramatically over short periods of time, thereby driving much of the total return performance.

This is why I employ an opportunistic capital recycling strategy with value investing principles when investing in high-yield stocks, because their growth tends to be even lower than normal. A significant portion of the total return torque over short periods is driven by valuation multiples expanding and contracting.

Meanwhile, over the medium term, returns on invested capital tend to have the highest impact. This is because the longer your duration goes, your holding duration goes, the more that growth will have an impact on your total returns, both in terms of actually driving earnings per share and dividends per share higher, but also in terms of justifying a higher valuation multiple. Therefore, if you can find a business that generates high returns on invested capital, it typically makes for a pretty solid medium-term holding of at least three to five years, as long as you're not significantly overpaying for it.

Finally, for very long-term holds, management quality tends to have the biggest impact on the total returns it generates. This is because over such long periods of time, valuation multiple changes tend to be relatively insignificant because they will eventually settle on a fair value range, and at that point, they will often not move much at all over a large period of time. Additionally, while these changes in invested capital that are outsized can have a significant impact over a short to medium term on the company's growth rate and valuation multiple, eventually the rest of the industry will likely play catch-up, or even a new technology will come that will disrupt that outsized return opportunity, rendering it relatively insignificant over the long term in terms of determining a company's total returns.

However, if the management is excellent and can innovate by continually finding new ways to generate high returns on invested capital and add new growth verticals to the business, the company will continue to be a very strong compounder of high returns on invested capital for many years into the future while also likely awarding a higher valuation multiple than they would have otherwise. As a result, when looking for core holdings to buy and hold for the long term, I put much more effort into assessing the quality of the company's management and its ability to innovate by adding new growth verticals to the business rather than simply obsessing over the current valuation or even return on invested capital metrics.

Of course, many of the businesses I invest in are not ones that I would ever consider holding forever. Instead, they're quality, defensive business models that are boring and simple and do not lend themselves to innovation, and therefore the types that I buy for short to medium term hold durations, and employ my opportunistic capital recycling strategy with. However, for the few that I may consider holding for the long term and not obsessing over valuations when purchasing, innovation and management are the keys to driving long-term investing success.

With this in mind, in this article, I'm going to share three dividend growth stocks (SCHD) that I would pick if I could only own three of them for the long term.

Why Gold Exposure Matters in a Long-term Portfolio

The first one I would discuss is Agnico Eagle Mines Limited (AEM). The reason I would pick AEM is simply that I am extremely bullish on gold (GLD) over the long term due to the accelerating de-dollarization trend, as well as major fiscal and geopolitical risks that I see rising from the U.S. government's runaway spending and debt, as well as the growing risk of a major conflict with China over Taiwan. All three of these trends I see as being very bullish for gold.

Therefore, if I could only own three stocks and had to hold them for a long time, I would want one of them to be in the gold industry. Moreover, since my goal is dividend growth, I would also want to own AEM, since it grows its intrinsic value per share and ultimately its dividends per share over time.

In fact, it stands out as one of the very rare gold mining stocks to consistently grow production on a per-share basis. Since 2000, it has grown its production from just 174,000 ounces to about 3.7 million ounces of gold today, which is akin to about 20 times growth. Additionally, it has consistently grown reserves per share, production per share, and free cash flow per share over time.

It also has very high-quality assets that should stand the test of time, with about 95% of its portfolio coming from tier 1 jurisdictions like Canada, Australia, Finland, and Mexico, while it also has over 15 years of reserve life at current production levels, which makes it a good long-term hold investment that should be able to remain in the game for plenty of time to enjoy further upside as my bullish thesis on gold continues to play out.

Management has shown that it is able to continually add value to the company over the long term by keeping costs under control and continually driving strong growth on a per-share basis, both through its aforementioned operational efficiencies as well as through growth projects. The company has also paid dividends very consistently with no dividend cuts over the past decade and significant dividend growth from $0.03 per share in 2004 to $1.60 per share today.

The company also has a good capital allocation policy that is very friendly to shareholders, as it pays out a base dividend that is intentionally conservative, making it very sustainable and likely to grow over time, while using excess free cash flow to buy back shares, pay out special dividends, and/or pay down debt and invest in growth opportunities. The company's balance sheet is also in exceptional shape, which should support continued dividend growth over time with a net cash position.

The AI Dividend Growth Machine Few Investors Appreciate

The next dividend growth stock that I include on this list is Broadcom (AVGO). The reason I would want AVGO in my portfolio is that it is not only a phenomenal dividend growth machine, but it is also a leading player in the AI boom. Given that AI is likely going to be one of the dominant macro trends in the coming years and decades, if I could only hold three stocks and I had to hold them for a long time, I would want to have AVGO included on that list.

One of the biggest reasons is that the company has a phenomenal AI backlog of about $73 billion that is expected to be delivered over the next 18 months, which is quite significant given that its AI semiconductor revenue guidance for Q1 FY2026 alone is $8.2 billion, which was already 100% year-over-year growth. The company is also well-positioned to benefit as the AI revolution progresses because it has a strong position in Ethernet switching, co-packaged optics, and custom AI accelerators, which means that it should benefit from ongoing AI investments.

The company also has proven itself to be highly innovative and able to maintain its leadership position while also driving strong growth and profitability across its diversified business model. Its semiconductor business benefits from custom silicon design-ins and high switching costs, while its software business, which consists of legacy assets as well as its VMware business, is generating very substantial revenue at $27 billion per year at very high profit margins of just under 80%.

The cash flows are also recurring and subscription-based, which makes them highly durable. This enables it to generate phenomenal free cash flow, given that its CapEx is only about 1% of its revenue. In fact, in Q4 2025 alone, it generated $7.5 billion of free cash flow, which gives it about a 90% cash conversion rate from operating cash flow to free cash flow.

This enables it to deleverage its balance sheet while also continuing to grow its dividend. In fact, it's grown its dividend for 15 straight years while also buying back stock and paying down debt. With a strong position in the AI boom, a proven ability to allocate capital effectively and innovate to remain a leader in its businesses, and its highly free cash flow generative business model and emphasis on dividend growth, with a five-year dividend per share CAGR of 12.63%, AVGO would be a company I would feel highly confident holding for the long term.

Infrastructure and Real Assets as a Long-Term Compounder

The third stock on my list would be Brookfield Asset Management (BAM). This is because it completes the trifecta of exposure to major macro themes that I have high conviction in over the long term, with AEM serving as the gold vehicle that hedges against a weakening dollar and rising geopolitical risks, AVGO serving as a dividend growth play that is benefiting significantly from the AI boom, and BAM serving as levered exposure to the ongoing infrastructure and real asset boom.

With about $1.15 trillion in assets under management and strong fundraising momentum, BAM is competitively positioned as one of the world's largest alternative asset managers and arguably its greatest infrastructure investor to benefit from the tremendous global need for infrastructure. In fact, it has about 47% of its assets under management in real assets and real estate, 45% in private credit, and 8% in private equity.

In its real assets and real estate businesses, it focuses heavily on data and AI-related infrastructure, midstream, regulated utilities, transportation, including ports, railroads, toll roads, and shipping containers, and finally world-class real estate assets in leading cities like New York and London. It also has exposure to renewable power, including a large hydro portfolio, nuclear, solar, wind, battery storage, and other similar assets.

In addition to its broad offerings and infrastructure that are positioned to benefit from the AI capex boom, as well as the rising need for reliable quality infrastructure in the developing and developed world, BAM is a fundraising machine that expects to see distributable earnings growth at a rapid clip in the years to come. Management believes they can generate 18% distributable earnings per share growth for years to come.

This will be fueled by their highly sticky fee-related earnings from its elongated funds, high retention rates, strong investment performance, and management's ability to continually add new investment strategies at the right time.

In addition to BAM's ability to grow its existing strategies organically at high returns on invested capital, it has also strategically added new strategies through acquisitions over time, including its acquisition of Oaktree Capital to drive its private credit business, which has since grown rapidly. We expect it to continue being able to do that over time to maintain a competitive posture in the market and continue to drive strong assets under management and fee-related earnings growth over time.

Given the asset-light nature of the business model as well, it should be able to consistently return a high percentage of its earnings to shareholders as dividends and buybacks, especially given that it has a very strong balance sheet with an A- credit rating from S&P, $2.6 billion of corporate liquidity, and a relatively low amount of long-dated debt, including 30-year senior unsecured notes that were issued in Q3 2025.

Risks

No investment is risk-free, and AEM, AVGO, and BAM - despite their many strengths - are no different. AEM's biggest risk is gold price volatility. While it mitigates much of this risk through its high-quality assets and consistently impressive operating efficiencies, a sharp correction in gold, which would not be surprising given gold's incredibly strong price run, would make a material dent in AEM's profits and likely its stock price as well. Additionally, AEM will need to continue doing a good job of executing on its operations and growth projects, which is easier said than done, given the complexity and capital intensity of the mining sector. Finally, AEM will need to continue doing a good job of keeping cost inflation under control. This risk will be especially acute if oil prices rise again, as diesel fuel is one of AEM's major input costs.

As for AVGO, its main risks lie in its concentrated customer base for its AI revenue, which means that changes in AI-related spending could have outsized impacts on near-term results. Additionally, the AI CapEx boom could come to a screeching halt if returns on investment falter. This would also likely materially impair AVGO's business. Therefore, if you lack conviction in AI as a transformative technology that will have a significant impact on the economy for decades to come, AVGO is likely a poor investment pick. The company has been driven to greatness in large part by the skill of its CEO (Hock Tan). Therefore, a change in leadership at some point in the distant future could be a risk to monitor. Finally, there is always competitive pressure in the technology sector, including from the likes of Nvidia (NVDA) in the Ethernet and custom solutions spaces, so AVGO must continue to innovate to defend its strong position within its niches.

Finally, BAM's stock price and earnings growth are highly sensitive to fundraising cyclicality. If the economy enters a downturn, its stock price will likely fall considerably, and its fundraising pace will slow. That said, its underlying earnings - which will still likely take a hit in such a scenario - tend to be much more stable, making its dividend likely sustainable in a recession, and it has a significant amount of dry powder that it could deploy in that environment at what will likely be very attractive valuations, setting it up for better long-term results for clients and shareholders. BAM is also sensitive to long-term interest rates rising due to the long-duration nature of the cash flows that its underlying assets generate. However, many of these assets also have inflation-linked escalators on their contracts, making them reasonably well hedged against such a scenario, since long-term interest rates often go up with inflation and vice versa. Finally, BAM does face competition from other alternative asset managers like Blackstone (BX), KKR (KKR), and others, which could lead to weakness in fundraising growth and/or fee compression moving forward. While BAM has staked out a strong competitive position in the infrastructure and real estate sectors in particular, there is no guarantee that it will not have to slash fees over time in order to retain market share.

Investor Takeaway

While none of these stocks have high current yields, with AVGO clocking in at just a 0.81% yield, AEM at a 0.75% yield, and BAM at a 3.4% yield, they all should be able to grow their dividends at double-digit annualized rates over time with very low risk of dividend cuts. Meanwhile, they are all well-positioned to benefit from major macro trends, making them phenomenal long-term wealth compounders that also should spin off an ever-growing stream of passive income.

Eventually, if they can sustain high rates of dividend growth for many years, as I expect them to, the yield on current cost should be quite substantial several decades from now. While I pursue an opportunistic capital recycling strategy and thereby generate 7–8% yields, total return outperformance, and low beta in my portfolios at High Yield Investor, these are some attractive positions to hold alongside such a strategy to provide more long-term, tax-efficient compounding for a total return-oriented dividend growth portfolio.

data.title: Live Nation: Lawsuit Uncertainty And Lofty Valuations Make Me Pass On It
data.description:
Introduction

Live Nation Entertainment, Inc. (LYV), parent company of Ticketmaster, has been in the news lately due to some developments regarding the lawsuits, so I thought I’d go through its performance over the last while to see if maybe this uncertainty provides an opportunity to invest. Unfortunately, even now the company seems to be very expensive, and on top of the uncertainty, I don’t see myself owning this company any time soon, unless its profitability increases substantially, which I don’t see happening right now.

Briefly on Performance

Starting from the top, LYV’s sales for the last year began to rebound after most of 2024 being in a downtrend. It is quite typical to see such a performance, as most people tend to go to a lot more outdoor/indoor events and concerts during the summer season rather than the cold seasons. Overall, however, the company has managed to grow its sales numbers every year at a decent pace since the bottom of the pandemic.

In terms of profitability and efficiency, the company operates in a very tight-margin business. The company’s operating expenses are consistently well above 85%, and most recently as much as 90% of the total revenues, so it is not unexpected to see the company making a loss in the less busy seasons of the year, like winter and after the busy summer. Margins across the board have been rather consistent over the last while, which is better than a decline.

Looking at other key efficiency metrics, we can see that the company’s ROA is nothing to write home about, while ROE is very elevated, which stems from the company’s massive financial leverage of around 20x, making ROE look very attractive when it is not the case at all. Additionally, the company’s ROTC is not bad. I would like to see it over 10% in the future, which would tell me the company has quite a bit of pricing power and a strong moat, and that makes sense because Ticketmaster has most of the market.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial position. As of Q3 ’25, LYV had $6.75B in cash and equivalents against $6.1B in long-term debt. As I alluded to earlier, the company does seem to have a decent amount of debt on its books, but it also has a mountain of cash, so I don’t think it’s a problem. The interest coverage ratio is around 6x, and net debt to EBITDA is around 3.5x, which is well within the company’s debt covenants. Also, the company’s cash flow from operations was over $1.4B so far this year, much higher than last year’s $680m by Q3 ’24. So, I think it is safe to say that the company has no liquidity or insolvency issues.

Overall, the company’s operations have been chugging along with very few improvements over the last while, especially when looking at its profitability. Revenue growth has been tremendous since the pandemic lows, but now that we have left that world behind, the demand has somewhat subsided from the massive demand spike when everything opened back up. Demand has normalized, and it will be interesting to see what is going to happen going forward.

Comments on the Outlook

Upcoming Quarter

The company didn’t provide any specific numbers for Q4, and that is most likely because of seasonality; however, we can gauge the performance through what the analysts are expecting. They expect the company to make a loss of -$1.04 on an adjusted basis and -$0.94 on a GAAP basis, on around $6.13B in sales. Compared to the same quarter last year, the company is much deeper in the hole for the upcoming quarter, which is a little concerning, but this can happen during such seasonal weakness, only for the operational performance to come roaring back in the strong seasons.

The management expects to see acceleration in fan growth for the full year and maintains its double-digit AOI growth, too.

Recent Lawsuit Developments

Back in 2024, the DOJ and many states in the U.S. filed a lawsuit against Live Nation, which owns Ticketmaster, to break them up due to an alleged illegal monopoly in live events, which pushes prices higher for fans.

Just last Friday (Jan 23rd), the court hearing on the DOJ’s antitrust lawsuit ended without a resolution, which boosted the company’s shares on the day. Live Nation wants to dismiss the case without a trial, saying that the DOJ lacks evidence of harm, with only a few isolated cases, and no monopoly has been proven. So, this was probably a positive development, but it is far from certain. The overhang persists, and the breakup could still be imminent if the judge denies it. The trial date remains set for March 2nd, 2026, according to the original documents.

It is hard to tell what’s going to happen and how the company is going to be affected in the future, but this uncertainty will continue to make LYV’s share price volatile until we see some sort of resolution, whether that is good or bad for LYV in the end. So, a couple of more turbulent months ahead.

Is the company a buy at these levels?

I was going to do a DCF analysis on the company, but seeing how analysts’ expectations in EPS are still pointing to the company’s FW P/E ratio in 2030 to be 38x at this share price, I know for a fact my model will say the company is grossly overvalued and is not a good buy. The company is just not growing its profitability fast enough to make the multiples contract at a reasonable pace. Sure, growth in EPS for 2027 looks good, but that still makes the company’s FW P/E ratio almost 44x. It’s just not appealing when the company’s top-line growth is under 10% a year on average.

Maybe you could say the reason it is trading at such premiums is because of its perceived dominance in the ticketing space. However, even that is in the air right now if the cases against LYV go to court and they end up breaking up.

I don’t think it is worth putting any money into the company right now. Ever since the lawsuits back in 2024, the company’s shares have gone nowhere, and I believe this will continue until we see some sort of resolution.

data.title: Meta Pre-Earnings: Capex Fears Equal Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) has gone out of the market’s favor, and it’s looking attractive pre-earnings. The stock is down around 7% over the past 6 months. The pullback reflects a growing skepticism about the cost of its long-term ambitions and the underwhelming response to its Llama 4 model. While Meta’s ad business remains its bread and butter, the market is currently fixated on two money pits: the massive capex required for AI infrastructure and Reality Labs' ongoing losses.

But the company isn’t standing on the side through this. Meta, as of early January, is reportedly cutting roughly 10% of its Reality Labs workforce (over 1,000 jobs) to stop the bleeding and shift resources toward more immediate wins, its AI infrastructure buildout. Meta also unveiled a massive initiative to build "tens of gigawatts" of AI infrastructure this decade through the "Meta Compute" initiative. Announced in mid-Jan, this project aims to centralize the oversight of Meta’s global data center fleet and supplier networks as CEO Mark Zuckerberg pivots the company toward the pursuit of personal superintelligence.

The bottleneck here is energy, but Meta is securing its energy future through 20-year agreements to buy power from Vistra (VST) nuclear plants and exploring the development of small modular reactors.

Past Coverage:

I last covered Meta in late July, after the company reported its Q2 '25. Despite the company beating expectations at the time and the stock surging over 12%, I saw limited upside ahead as I saw rising infrastructure and AI talent costs and increasing competitive pressures weighing on the stock. Meta is down over 13.7% since my downgrade, but I think it’s time to look at Meta again.

I’ve been a buy on Meta since late September of 2024, and it has been on a wild ride since then. The stock not only hit an all-time high of $795 in mid-August of last year. Since then, Meta has been in a free fall, and I think we’re nearing the bottom. Meta is underperforming the market and its peers in the Mag 7 over the past year, up around 1.2%.

Investment Thesis:

At current levels, I look at Meta as a buy on weakness into earnings, not because the spending story is resolved, but because expectations have reset enough that even incremental clarity and discipline could lead to a sentiment shift. Going into print, I’m looking at a couple of things:

The 2026 spending floor takes center stage for me. We know by now that Meta’s 2025 capex is at around $70-$72 billion, and 4Q25 earnings will give us more color on FY26 spending. According to the company’s CFO Susan Li on the Q3 '25 earnings call,

“As a result, our current expectation is that CapEx dollar growth will be notably larger in 2026 than 2025. We also anticipate total expenses will grow at a significantly faster percentage rate in 2026 than 2025, with growth primarily driven by infrastructure costs, including incremental cloud expenses and depreciation.”

While increased capex isn’t an issue for Alphabet (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN), or Microsoft (MSFT), investors have grown wary of Meta’s spending, mainly due to the cash burn in Zuckerberg’s Reality Labs. Now, investors have Meta Compute to worry about, and the question of whether that will erode free cash flow or not is being floated around. I think management needs to show a clear path going forward; the spending will need to hit a ceiling, and the question is now whether we’re approaching that ceiling or still in the early innings of a multi-year spend cycle.

Valuation:

Meta isn’t expensive at current levels. On a relative valuation basis, it trades at a forward PEG ratio of 1.27, compared with 2.10 for Microsoft, 2.90 for Apple (AAPL), 2.02 for Google, and 1.81 for Amazon, while Nvidia (NVDA) is at 1.06 and Tesla (TSLA) sits at an extreme 10.72. On forward EV/Sales, Meta trades at 8.4x, cheaper than Microsoft at 10.7x and Google at 9.8x, and roughly in line with Apple at 8.0x, while sitting at a steep discount to Nvidia at 21.1x and Tesla at 15.5x. For a company still growing revenue around 20% with a highly profitable core ad business, Meta’s valuation reflects a disproportionate penalty for capex and Reality Labs, rather than deterioration in fundamentals, in my opinion.

When layering in growth, Meta’s valuation discount looks even harder to justify. Meta is delivering 21.3% trailing revenue growth and 20.4% forward revenue growth, outpacing Microsoft, Apple, Google, and Amazon, and trailing only Nvidia’s AI-driven surge. On earnings, Meta is expected to grow diluted EPS 25.9% forward, materially faster than Microsoft, Apple, and Google, and broadly competitive with Amazon.

The stock just broke EMA200 at $662 and is holding, currently trading at $672. The recent low near $580–585 now looks like a confirmed bottom, and the EMA21 and EMA50 remain to act as support. The RSI around 60 signals strengthening bullish momentum without being overbought, leaving room for upside.

Earnings Preview:

Expectations for Meta are muted going into print, and I think a lot of the negatives are priced in. Consensus is calling for revenue of $58.3 billion, a 20% to 21% year-over-year growth, with adjusted EPS of $8.16, a 2% growth during the same period.

Meta has outpaced these growth rates in both revenue and earnings over the past two quarters, so the bar isn’t high. The company is also expected to provide revenue guidance for Q1 '26, with consensus around $51.3 billion in revenue, representing 21% year-over-year growth, and EPS of $6.29. Even if Q1 guidance comes in softer than expected, I don’t see a significant downside on that since seasonality tells us that Q4 is typically a strong quarter due to holiday advertising spend.

Bank of America is more optimistic and sees Q4 revenue of $59.2 billion and EPS of $8.27, driven by a healthy macroeconomic backdrop and AI-targeting capabilities. The firm also expects Q1 '26 revenue of $52.3 billion and EPS of $6.31 and for Meta to guide for $50 billion to $52.5 billion during the same period. BofA also sees FY26 expenses at $153 billion to $160 billion, which would represent 30% to 36% growth year over year, against consensus at $150 billion, and capex at $109 billion to $114 billion, during the same period, against consensus at $110 billion.

Other metrics I’m looking at are correlated to Meta’s ad business, which are 1. ad impressions and 2. Average price per ad. So, even if Meta can exceed modest revenue expectations and show discipline in FY26 spending, I think we could see an upside surprise on print. With the 10% cut to Reality Labs, the narrative has clearly shifted, and following the success of the Ray-Ban Meta glasses, which have sold over 2 million units since 2023, investors are wondering whether the downsizing is in favor of AI wearables, which are a high-margin business.

I'm bullish on Meta going into print and would recommend investors take advantage of the pullback.

data.title: Weekly Treasury Simulation, January 23, 2026: Most Likely 3-Month Bill Rate Shows Steady Decline
data.description:
As explained in Prof. Robert Jarrow’s book cited below, forward rates contain a risk premium above and beyond the market’s expectations for the 3-month forward rate. We document the size of that risk premium in this graph, which shows the zero-coupon yield curve implied by current Treasury prices compared with the annualized compounded yield on 3-month Treasury bills that market participants would expect based on the daily movement of government bond yields in 14 countries since 1962. The risk premium, the reward for a long-term investment, is large and widens over most of the full 30-year maturity range. The graph also shows a decline in expected yields at a steady pace for the full 30 years. We explain the details below.

For more on this topic, see the analysis of government bond yields in 14 countries through November 30, 2025 given in the appendix.

Inverted Yields, Negative Rates, and U.S. Treasury Probabilities 10 Years Forward

The negative 2-year/10-year Treasury spread ended on August 26, 2024 after 537 trading days. The spread is currently at positive 0.64%. The table below shows that the August 26, 2024 streak of inverted yield curves is the longest in the U.S. Treasury market since the 2-year Treasury yield was first reported on June 1, 1976. The second longest streak is 423 trading days starting on August 18, 1978.

In this week’s forecast, the focus is on three elements of interest rate behavior: the future probability of the recession-predicting inverted yield curve, the probability of negative rates, and the probability distribution of U.S. Treasury yields over the next decade.

We start from the closing U.S. Treasury yield curve published daily by the U.S. Department of the Treasury. Using a maximum smoothness forward rate approach, Friday’s implied forward rate curve shows the shortest maturity 1-month forward rates at 3.78%, versus 3.75% last week. There is a decline followed by a gradual rise until rates peak at 5.91%, compared to 5.97% the previous week. Forward rates stabilize at 4.59% at the 30-year horizon, versus 4.56% last week.

Using the methodology outlined in the appendix, we simulate 50,000 future paths for the U.S. Treasury yield curve out to thirty years. The next three sections summarize our conclusions from that simulation.

Inverted Treasury Yields: Positive Spread Now, 24.7% Probability by January 6, 2040

Many economists have concluded that a downward sloping U.S. Treasury yield curve is an important indicator of future recessions. A recent example is this paper by Alex Domash and Lawrence H. Summers. We measure the probability that the 10-year par coupon Treasury yield is lower than the 2-year par coupon Treasury for every scenario in each of the first 40 quarterly periods in the simulation.[1] The next graph shows that the probability of an inverted yield now peaks at 24.7%, unchanged from one week before, in the 91-day period ending January 6, 2040.

Negative Treasury Bill Yields: 11.3% Probability by December 27, 2047

The next graph describes the probability of negative 3-month Treasury bill rates for all but the first 3 months of the next three decades. The probability of negative rates starts near zero and then peaks at 11.3% in the 91-day period ending December 27, 2047.

Calculating the Default Risk from Interest Rate Maturity Mismatches

In light of the interest-rate-risk-driven failure of Silicon Valley Bank on March 10, 2023, we have added a table that applies equally well to banks, institutional investors, and individual investor mismatches from buying long-term Treasury bonds with borrowed short-term funds. We assume that the sole asset is a 10-year Treasury bond purchased at time zero at par value of $100. We analyze default risk for four different initial market value of equity to market value of asset ratios: 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%. For the banking example, we assume that the only class of liabilities is deposits that can be withdrawn at par at any time. In the institutional and retail investor case, we assume that the liability is essentially a borrowing on margin/repurchase agreement with the possibility of margin calls. For all investors, the amount of liabilities (95, 90, 85 or 80) represents a “strike price” on a put option held by the liability holders. Failure occurs via a margin call, bank run, or regulatory takeover (in the banking case) when the value of assets falls below the value of liabilities.

The graph below shows the cumulative 10-year probabilities of failure for each of the 4 possible capital ratios when the asset’s maturity is 10 years. For the 5 percent case, that default probability is 48.97%, compared to 49.18% from last week.

This default probability analysis is updated weekly based on the U.S. Treasury yield simulation described in the next section. The calculation process is the same for any portfolio of assets with credit risk included.

U.S. Treasury Probabilities 10 Years Forward

In this section, the focus turns to the decade ahead. This week’s simulation shows that the most likely range for the 3-month U.S. Treasury bill yield in ten years is from 1% to 2%, unchanged from last week. There is a 22.24% probability that the 3-month yield falls in this range, compared to 22.26% one week before. For the 10-year Treasury yield, the most likely range is from 2% to 3%, unchanged from last week. The probability of being in this range is 21.23%, compared to 21.18% one week prior.

In a recent post on SeekingAlpha, we pointed out that a forecast of “heads” or “tails” in a coin flip leaves out critical information. What a sophisticated bettor needs to know is that, on average for a fair coin, the probability of heads is 50%. A forecast that the next coin flip will be “heads” is literally worth nothing to investors because the outcome is purely random.

The same is true for interest rates.

In this section we present the detailed probability distribution for both the 3-month Treasury bill rate and the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield 10 years forward using semi-annual time steps.[2] We present the probability of where rates will be at each time step in one percent “rate buckets.” The forecast for 3-month Treasury yields is shown in this graph:

3-Month U.S. Treasury Yield Data Are Attached.

SAS3monthUST20260123.xlsx

The probability that the 3-month Treasury bill yield will be between 1% and 2% in 2 years is shown in column 4: 25.12%. The probability that the 3-month Treasury bill yield will be negative (as it has been often in Europe and Japan) in 2 years is 1.68% plus 0.05% plus 0.00% = 1.73% (difference due to rounding). Cells shaded in blue represent positive probabilities of occurring, but the probability has been rounded to the nearest 0.01%. The shading scheme is defined as follows:

  • Dark blue: the probability is greater than 0% but less than 1%
  • Light blue: the probability is greater than or equal to 1% and less than 5%
  • Light yellow: the probability is greater than or equal to 5% and 10%
  • Medium yellow: the probability is greater than or equal to 10% and less than 20%
  • Orange: the probability is greater than or equal to 20% and less than 25%
  • Red: the probability is greater than 25%

The chart below shows the same probabilities for the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield derived as part of the same simulation.

10-Year US Treasury Yield Data Are Attached.

SAS10yearUST20260123.xlsx

Modeling Correlated Foreign Exchange Rates and Government Yields

The SAS HJM++© methodology is based on five decades of research by Prof. Robert Jarrow. Using the no-arbitrage conditions of Heath, Jarrow and Morton [1992] and foreign exchange rate no-arbitrage conditions from Amin and Jarrow [1992], foreign exchange rates and government yields in major markets are simulated jointly. The simulation is based on daily movements in government yields and foreign exchange rates since January 2, 1962. The resulting distribution of the yen-U.S. dollar exchange rate one year forward is shown in this graph:

Similarly, the forward-looking correlation between 10-year zero-coupon Bund and U.S. Treasury yields is shown below:

For more information on HJM++ scenario generation and no-arbitrage Monte Carlo simulation, please contact the author.

Appendix: Treasury Simulation Methodology

The Treasury yield probabilities are derived using the same methodology that SAS Institute Inc. recommends to its KRIS® and SAS ALM/Kamakura Risk Manager® clients. A moderately technical explanation is given later in the appendix, but we summarize it in plain English first.

Step 1: We take the closing U.S. Treasury yield curve as our starting point.

Step 2: We use the number of points on the yield curve that best explains historical yield curve shifts. Using daily government bond yield data from 14 countries from 1962 through November 30, 2025, we conclude that 12 “factors” drive almost all movements of government bond yields. The countries on which the analysis is based are Australia, Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, New Zealand. Russia, Singapore, Spain, Sweden, Thailand, the United Kingdom, and the United States of America. No data from Russia is included after January, 2022. The graph below shows which points on the zero-coupon yield curve are incorporated as risk factors and the order in which they are added to the model:

Step 3: We measure the volatility of changes in those factors and how volatility has changed over the same period.

Step 4: Using those measured volatilities, we generate 50,000 random shocks at each time step and derive the resulting yield curve.

Step 5: We “validate” the model to make sure that the simulation EXACTLY prices the starting Treasury curve and that it fits history as well as possible. The methodology for doing this is described below.

Step 6: We take all 50,000 simulated yield curves and calculate the probabilities that yields fall in each of the 1% “buckets” displayed in the graph.

Do Treasury Yields Accurately Reflect Expected Future Inflation?

We showed in a recent post on SeekingAlpha that, on average, investors have almost always done better by buying long term bonds than by rolling over short term Treasury bills. That means that market participants have generally (but not always) been accurate in forecasting future inflation and adding a risk premium to that forecast.

The distribution above helps investors estimate the probability of success from going long.

Finally, as mentioned weekly in the Corporate Bond Investor Friday overview, the future expenses (both the amount and the timing) that all investors are trying to cover with their investments are an important part of investment strategy. The author follows his own advice: cover the short-term cash needs first and then step out to cover more distant cash needs as savings and investment returns accumulate.

Technical Details

Daily government bond yields from the 14 countries listed above form the base historical data for fitting the number of yield curve factors and their volatility. The U.S. historical data is provided by the U.S. Department of the Treasury. The use of the international bond data increases the number of observations to more than 109,000 and provides a more complete range of experience with both high rates and negative rates than a U.S. data set alone provides.

The modeling process was published in a very important paper by David Heath, Robert Jarrow and Andrew Morton in 1992:

For technically inclined readers, we recommend Prof. Jarrow’s book Modeling Fixed Income Securities and Interest Rate Options for those who want to know exactly how the “HJM” model construction works.

The number of factors, 12 for the 14-country model, has been stable since June 30, 2017.

Footnotes:

[1] After the first 20 years in the simulation, the 10-year Treasury cannot be derived from the initial 30 years of Treasury yields.

[2] The full simulation uses 91-day time steps for 30 years forward. This note summarizes just the first 10 years of the full simulation.

data.title: When Everyone Feared The Shutdown, I Saw An Opportunity, And It Could Happen Again
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate my buy recommendation on assets that track the main American indices. This article is part of my weekly series on macroeconomics. My intention with this article is to analyze the strong increase in the chances of a new government shutdown in the current market context.

New Government Shutdown?

SA recently reported the odds of a government shutdown increasing to 80%, as Senate Democrats promised to block $1.2 trillion in funding, which includes funds for the Department of Homeland Security.

My intention here is to analyze what happens in the market and in investments based on these facts. It is important to highlight that the last shutdown came to an end in mid-November and lasted 43 days (the longest in history). It is worth remembering that the center of the debate over the last shutdown was due to the health sector. Democrats refused to pass the bill without expanding health care programs. Therefore, the debate may occur again for a new reason.

Impacts If a New Government Shutdown Occurs

As I already said in another article, there is no statistical data that proves a worsening in the stock market due to a Government shutdown, on the contrary, this is an opportunity.

As you can see from the history, there is an average gain of 12.7% in the market when this event occurs, and it is worth remembering that the last one in 2025 did not prevent a 15.7% rise in the S&P 500.

Therefore, you should not mix political issues with investments. The entire economy and business environment of the United States is much greater than the influence of one political party in power, and the graph below shows this clearly.

In my opinion, you should focus on where the main investors are investing their capital to make your decisions, so let's see more insights.

Investment Flow

Investors are confident in the American market. A GS tool that measures net weekly global flows detected the largest cash movement for shares in recent years. About $133 billion was invested in stocks by investors.

But to be successful in investing with this type of information, you need to understand where this capital is going. That said, the following graph shows a very relevant insight.

About 63% of S&P 500 companies are beating the index year to date. This is the best market share since 2021. This data shows that the increase is not concentrated, which is very good.

The momentum of S&P 500 companies against small caps also presents a different dynamic. The chart below shows that we have seen the IWM outperform the S&P 500 for 15 consecutive days. This pattern has not occurred since 2008 and indicates a rotation in the market.

I remain optimistic about the S&P 500 in 2026, and I confess that this dynamic of returns not concentrated in a few companies makes me even more confident. Now, let's analyze some data about the upcoming earnings season.

Earnings Season Insights

A very relevant insight on this topic is depicted in the graphs below. Although the magnificent seven contributed to the S&P 500 return in 2025, this contribution was not as large as many investors believe. It is curious that when we look with a magnifying glass, the profit growth of the magnificent seven compared to the other 493 companies should drop from Q4 to Q1. Is your portfolio prepared for this, reader?

I reiterate my buy recommendation on assets that track the main American indices. My target for the S&P 500 at the end of 2026 is 7787 points regardless of a new government shutdown. It's worth remembering that I also offer my recommended portfolio of stocks for 2026 that has great dividend-paying potential; year to date, the portfolio rises 2.92% while the S&P 500 rises 1.02%.

The Bottom Line

The incident in Minneapolis was tragic, but I am here in SA to analyze the impacts of any investment situation. In this sense, the event made the bets of a new government shutdown soar to around 80%. As has been seen and proven, there is nothing that indicates a worsening in the investment scenario due to a new government shutdown; in fact, the history indicates strong gains in the stock market.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate my buy recommendation on assets that track the main American indices. In my opinion, expectations of a new government shutdown are an opportunity, especially among companies other than the magnificent seven.

data.title: Bank of Hawaii: Strong Metric Improvement
data.description:
Today, we continue our Q4 regional bank coverage with a real specialized name in Bank of Hawaii Corporation (BOH). As the name implies, it serves businesses and consumers in the Hawaiian Islands. What we know about this region is that it is largely dependent on tourism. Well, travel has remained robust, as evidenced by recent earnings reports out of the airlines. Hawaii has largely fully bounced back from the terrible wildfires of a few years ago. Although this is a specialized economy being serviced, we like to cover a diverse set of regional banks to help us, as investors, get an understanding of strength (or weakness) in the underlying economy.

This bank, which we continue to rate a Hold, has just reported its Q4 earnings this morning. Let us discuss the key metrics we follow for banks for this specialized name.

Bank of Hawaii Q4 revenues, provisions for losses, and margins

The Hawaiian bank reported revenue of $189.7 million, a $4.8 million beat versus consensus expectations. Now, for a while, provisions for credit losses were increasing. Provisions offset earnings power. In the sequential Q3, provisions were $2.5 million. Here in Q4, they were still $2.5 million, and the good news is that this is down by $1.3 million from last year. Non-interest income dipped sequentially but was up from last year, which is a mixed result. Non-interest income was $44.3 million, down 3.7% from Q3 and up 2.8% from the comparable 2024 fourth quarter.

How about margins? Thus far, our coverage of Q4 regional bank earnings has shown a lot of strength in margins. The average yield on loans and leases was 4.81%, which was down 5 basis points from the sequential quarter and up 8 basis points from the same period last year. The average cost of interest-bearing deposits was 1.94%, down 20 basis points from Q3 and down 43 basis points from a year ago. So margins did expand to 2.61%, up 15 basis points from the sequential quarter, so this is positive. It was also up 42 basis points from a year ago. Overall, EPS was $1.39 for the quarter.

Bank of Hawaii deposits and loans

We also monitor trends in deposits and loans for any bank we examine, as these are key metrics for the bank's health. Here in Q4, total deposits were up by 0.5% from the sequential Q3 to $21.2 billion and were up 2.7% from a year ago. Total loans also increased 0.4% sequentially to $14.1 billion. Commercial loans dipped a slight 0.1% from Q3, while consumer loans were up 0.8%. Overall, this is a positive result.

Bank of Hawaii trends in asset quality

Perhaps not surprisingly, given the margin expansion and earnings, we saw improvement in the return metrics we follow. The return on average assets was 1.01%, rising from 0.88% in Q3. It is also up dramatically from 0.64% a year ago. The return on equity was 13.33%, rising 113 basis points from Q3, and was up from 9.78% last year. The efficiency ratio was the best it has been in years, hitting 57.75% and improving from 61.53% in Q3.

The overall asset quality remained strong here in Q4, folks. We saw non-performing assets were $14.2 million, down $2.7 million from Q3 and down $5.1 million from a year ago. Non-performing assets as a percentage of total loans were just 0.10%, down 2 basis points from Q3 and down 4 basis points from last year. Net charge-offs were $4.1 million, or 12 basis points annualized, of total average loans, although this was a slight blemish, as it was up $1.6 million, or 5 basis points, from Q3. However, the allowance for credit losses on loans dipped to $146.8 million, down $2.0 million from Q3. This was 1.04% of all loans, down 2 basis points from Q3. Overall, these were strong trends in asset quality.

Final thoughts

This was a great quarter for Bank of Hawaii Corporation overall. We have been at a market perform rating on this name for a while, and the stock has moved with the sector. Investors still get a 4% yield here. Margins are expanding, loans and deposits are growing, and asset quality has held up strong.

This report and our analysis add to the evidence base that there are no signs the economy is slowing down in any way. Further, borrowers are paying back loans with little issue.

data.title: Viridian Therapeutics: Cautious Optimism As Key Milestones Approach (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Today, I am putting Viridian Therapeutics, Inc. (VRDN) in the spotlight for the first time since my article on this promising biopharma concern in early June of last year. The stock has experienced a nice rally since then. The last piece about Viridian on Seeking Alpha noted the company had an eventful 2026 ahead of it. Therefore, it felt like it was a good time to circle back on Viridian. An updated analysis follows better.

Company Overview

Massachusetts-headquartered Viridian Therapeutics core focus is developing treatments for active and chronic thyroid eye disease, or TED. Just under 200,000 individuals in the United States are afflicted by these two conditions. The stock currently trades at just under $33.00 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of just north of $3 billion.

Pipeline Development

The company is moving two primary candidates through its pipeline. Actually, these are two different versions of the same base molecule. Both are targeting active and chronic TED. The molecule in question is called veligrotug. This is a monoclonal antibody that is targeting insulin-like growth factor-1 receptor. A biologic license application for Veligrotug was accepted by the FDA in late December for priority review. The application's PDUFA date is June 30, 2026.

Tepezza from Amgen (AMGN), which was approved in 2020, is the current standard of care for active and chronic TED. Tepezza is nearing a $2 billion annual run rate; it should be noted. Based on study results, veligrotug showed superiority to Tepezza in some key traits, including significantly shorter infusion times.

It should be noted that Viridian is already working on an improved version of veligrotug dubbed VRDN-003. The first data from two Phase 3 studies from the subcutaneously delivered VRDN-003 should be out in the first quarter of this year. Results from the other study should hit in the second quarter of 2026. VRDN-003 has four to five times the half-life of the current veligrotug candidate and shorter infusion times as well. If all goes well, VRDN-003 should be approved sometime in 2027.

Viridian Therapeutics has a couple of other candidates in very early-stage development that are not germane to this analysis.

In addition to some balance sheet activity, which is covered in the next section, and the recent BLA submission accepted by the FDA, there was one other significant news event since my last article on Viridian. That occurred in late July. The company signed an exclusive collaboration agreement around the development and marketing of Veligrotug and VRDN-003 in Japan with Kissei Pharmaceutical. Viridian received $70 million via an upfront payment once it inked this deal. The company can also earn up to $315 million in additional regulatory, developmental, and sales milestones as well as tiered royalties based on net sales in Japan, which will range from the 20s to mid-30s depending on performance.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet

The analyst community is universally optimistic around the prospects for Viridian Therapeutics. Since third-quarter results were posted, more than a dozen analyst firms, including Wedbush, Goldman Sachs, and RBC Capital, have posted Buy ratings on the stock.

Approximately nine percent of the outstanding float in the shares is currently held short. There has been no insider activity in the stock in 2026. In 2025, a beneficial owner purchased approximately $20 million worth of shares. Half of that during October's secondary offering. The Chief Legal Officer sold just over $70,000 worth of stock on the last day of 2025. That has been the only insider sale since June 2023. The company held approximately $490 million of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet at the end of the third quarter. Viridian raised an additional $250 million via a secondary offering in October. In addition, also in October, Viridian entered into a royalty financing agreement with DRI Healthcare Acquisitions LP. This will supply up to $300 million in total capital. This included a $55 million upfront payout and $115 million in near-term milestones tied to positive VRDN-003 topline data as well as FDA approval of veligrotug.

This is how management described its balance sheet as of October 31, inclusive of that capital raise and royalty agreement.

Existing cash, potential near-term milestones from the royalty agreement, and anticipated commercial revenues, if both veligrotug and VRDN-003 are approved, are expected to fund its current business plans through profitability.'

Conclusion

The stock of Viridian Therapeutics has more than doubled since I gave the shares a 'thumbs up' in early June. I still like this story from a long-term perspective. That said, VRDN is not the screaming Buy it was seven months ago.

Now, obviously, if the analyst community is right about the company's earnings growth over the next nearly decade, the shares obviously are a value over the long term. That said, Viridian is not projected to be profitable until FY2028 as revenues move sharply higher.

The stock is getting close to the low end of bullish analyst price targets and fully reflects its recent string of positive events covered in the sections above. Therefore, I am not chasing the rally as the stock could have a bout of profit taking if the overall market experiences a 'hiccup' in the months ahead. I also have a relatively large position in VRDN, mostly via covered call holdings. Now, as those covered call holdings expire in the money in the months ahead, and if VRDN moves back under $30 a share, I would replace those holdings with additional covered call orders. And that is my current 'game plan' around the stock of this company that appears to have a promising long-term future.
